For more than 200 years, while Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob lived freely, the Hebrews increased slowly; only about seventy people went down into Egypt. There, in about the same number of years, though under cruel slavery, they became a large nation. This wonderful increase was according to the promise long before made to the fathers. Though God's promises are sometimes slow to be fulfilled, they are always sure.

The land of Egypt became to Israel a house of bondage. The place where we have been happy may soon become the place of our affliction; and that may prove the greatest trial for us, which we said would comfort us. Cease from relying on people, and do not say of any place on this earth, This is my rest. All who knew Joseph loved him and were kind to his brothers for his sake; but the best and most useful services a person does for others are soon forgotten after death. Our greatest care should be to serve God and to please him who is not unrighteous, whatever people do, to forget our work and labor of love. Israel’s offense is that he prospers. There is no sight more hateful to a wicked person than the prosperity of the righteous. The Egyptians feared that the children of Israel would join their enemies and leave the land. Wickedness is always cowardly and unjust; it makes a person fear where there is no fear, and flee when no one pursues him. And human wisdom often is foolishness, and very sinful. God’s people had taskmasters set over them, not only to burden them but to afflict them with their burdens. They not only made them serve for Pharaoh's profit but so that their lives became bitter. The Israelites wonderfully increased. Christianity spread most when it was persecuted; the blood of the martyrs was the seed of the church. Those who take counsel against the Lord and his Israel do but imagine a vain thing and create greater trouble for themselves.

The Egyptians tried to destroy Israel by murdering their children. The enmity that is in the seed of the serpent, against the Seed of the woman, makes people forget all pity. It is plain that the Hebrews were now under an uncommon blessing. And we see that the services done for God’s Israel are often repaid in kind. Pharaoh gave orders to drown all the male children of the Hebrews. The enemy who, by Pharaoh, attempted to destroy the church in this its infant state, is busy to stifle the rise of serious reflections in the heart of man. Let those who would escape be afraid of sinning and cry directly and fervently to the Lord for assistance.
